behaviors.description: Out-of-stock products excluded from recommendations regardless of relevance score.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a logic bug in the availability filter — a product with a high relevance score passes through when it should be blocked. This is a single function boundary: given a list of (product, score) pairs and an availability predicate, the output must contain no unavailable products. No module handoff is required to expose this bug. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed; in-memory product stubs suffice. LLM-DEP: Filtering is deterministic. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would require the ranking engine to also be wired in; the behavior text ('excluded from recommendation results regardless of relevance score') describes the filter function's own invariant, not a cross-module handoff failure.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a list of products with mixed availability and pre-assigned relevance scores, where the highest-scoring product is out-of-stock. Pass the list through the availability filter and assert that the output contains zero out-of-stock products and that all in-stock products with their original scores are present.

behaviors.description: User preference filter correctly excludes explicitly blocked product categories.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a logic bug in the preference filter function: a product whose category is in the user's blocked list is returned anyway. This is a pure function (blocked_categories × product_catalog → filtered_catalog) with no module handoff needed to expose the bug. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network/DB needed. LLM-DEP: Deterministic. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior text describes a filter predicate's correctness, not an interaction between the preference store and the filtering pipeline; escalating to Integration is unwarranted unless the behavior described a failure at that boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user settings object with two blocked categories and a product list spanning three categories (one of which is double-blocked). Pass through the preference filter and assert: (a) all products in blocked categories are absent, (b) all products in the allowed category are present, (c) no products are duplicated.

behaviors.description: Recommendation algorithm ranks products matching recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only appears when the browsing history data pipeline and the ranking scorer are wired together — a unit test of the scorer alone with hardcoded inputs cannot verify that real browsing history signals are correctly retrieved and weighted. The bug is at the handoff between the browsing history repository and the ranking function. REAL-INFRA: No real network/OS needed; an in-process fake browsing store suffices. LLM-DEP: The description ('weighs purchase history, browsing patterns') describes a traditional algorithmic/statistical recommender, not an LLM; the ranking is deterministic given fixed inputs. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: no text indicates a real ML/AI model call whose output quality must be validated; the behavior tests that browsing signals are consumed and applied correctly, which a deterministic algorithm handles.
behaviors.test_description: Wire a fake browsing history store (user has viewed products A and B in category X) to the recommendation engine with a controlled product catalog. Run recommendations and assert that products in category X with browsing signal appear ranked above products in category Y with no browsing signal. Vary the number of browsing events and confirm ranking shifts accordingly.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page with stable ordering when underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Ordering instability typically emerges at the boundary between the ranking engine (which produces a scored list) and the pagination layer (which slices it). A unit test of the slicer alone with a pre-sorted list cannot detect non-determinism introduced upstream. The failure needs both modules to hand off. REAL-INFRA: No real DB needed; in-memory state captures the invariant. LLM-DEP: Pagination + sorting are deterministic. REJECTION-GATE for System: no real network or OS filesystem events are required; in-process fake data reproduces the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the recommendation engine with a fixed user state and catalog. Request page 2 twice consecutively with no intervening mutations. Assert the two responses are byte-identical (same product IDs in the same order). Also assert page 2 contains a disjoint set from page 1 and that page_size items are returned.

behaviors.description: Recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed (serendipity requirement).
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The serendipity constraint requires the browsing history store, the product catalog, and the diversity-injection component to cooperate. A unit test of diversity logic with hardcoded category lists cannot verify that unexplored categories are correctly derived from the live browsing profile. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed. LLM-DEP: Described as an explicit algorithmic diversity requirement, not a quality judgment that only a real ML model can make. The assertion is structural (at least one unvisited category present), not a quality assessment of recommendations. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: the behavior tests a hard constraint enforced by code, not an emergent quality property of an AI model.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a user profile with browsing history covering exactly two categories. Configure a catalog with products in four categories. Run the recommendation engine and assert that the result set contains at least one product whose category does not appear in the user's browsing history. Repeat with the user profile covering all-but-one category to verify the constraint still holds at the boundary.

behaviors.description: New user with no purchase or browsing history receives popularity-based recommendations, not an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The cold-start failure is a missing or broken fallback path triggered only when the user profile lookup returns empty history. The bug appears at the handoff between the profile store (returning null/empty) and the ranking engine (failing to fall back to popularity). A unit test with a hardcoded empty profile list misses failures in how the engine reacts to the empty signal from the real profile loader. REAL-INFRA: No real DB needed; a stub profile store returning an empty record suffices. LLM-DEP: Popularity ranking is deterministic (e.g., aggregate sales counts). REJECTION-GATE for System: no OS/network primitives required.
behaviors.test_description: Wire a stub user profile store that returns no purchase or browsing history for a new user ID. Inject a catalog with known popularity scores. Run recommendations and assert: (a) the result set is non-empty, (b) all returned products are ordered by descending popularity score, (c) no products from a personalised-signal path appear in the ranking.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records correct product ID, position in list, and timestamp for each recommendation interaction.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is incorrect data written to the tracking store — wrong product ID, wrong position index (off-by-one), or missing/wrong timestamp. This only manifests when the click event handler (which receives a click event with a product ID and list context) and the tracking store (which persists the record) are wired together. A unit test of the event handler alone with a mock store cannot verify the stored record's shape. REAL-INFRA: A real database is not required; an in-memory tracking store or a captured-write spy catches the failure. LLM-DEP: Tracking is deterministic. REJECTION-GATE for System: no real network/OS events required to expose incorrect field mapping.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the recommendation engine with a known result list of five products. Simulate a click event on the third product. Assert the tracking store received exactly one record with: product_id matching the third product, position=3 (or 2 if 0-indexed, per spec), and timestamp within an acceptable window of now. Then simulate two more clicks and assert three records in total with correct positions.

behaviors.description: Response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Latency under real load is the only meaningful measurement here. An in-process fake catalog of 100k items cannot reproduce I/O latency, memory pressure, serialization cost, or query planning behavior against a real data store. The failure — exceeding 200ms — only appears under real infrastructure with a real-sized dataset. MOCK-HIDE: A stub catalog always returns instantly, making this test vacuously pass and hiding every real performance bottleneck. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: the behavior text specifies 'a catalog of 100,000 products,' which requires real infrastructure to populate and query; an in-process fake would not expose real algorithmic or I/O scaling behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Provision a test catalog with 100,000 products in a real database (or populated in-memory structure exercising real query/sort paths). Execute the recommendation pipeline end-to-end for a representative user profile. Capture wall-clock time from request to fully-serialized response. Assert p99 latency < 200ms across N repeated calls. Run against both cold-cache and warm-cache scenarios.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure appears at the handoff between the purchase event pipeline (writing to purchase history) and the ranking engine (reading and applying the deprioritization rule). A unit test of the ranker with a hardcoded purchased-product list cannot detect failures where the purchase event is not correctly persisted or retrieved before the next recommendation call. REAL-INFRA: No real network/OS needed; an in-process fake purchase store captures the cross-module handoff. LLM-DEP: The deprioritization rule ('products the user has bought rank lower') is a deterministic signal applied to a traditional recommender. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: the behavior tests that a specific signal is correctly applied, not the quality of AI-generated rankings.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the recommendation engine with a user profile where product X has a high relevance score and has not been purchased. Capture the initial recommendation ranking (X appears near top). Simulate a purchase event for product X, flushing it to the fake purchase history store. Re-run recommendations and assert that product X's rank is lower than in the initial response, and lower than at least one product with equivalent relevance score but no purchase history.

behaviors.description: Recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — two consecutive requests with no intervening activity return identical ranked lists.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Non-determinism typically originates deep in the pipeline — hash-map iteration order, un-seeded random tie-breaking, timestamp-seeded shuffles for serendipity, or race conditions between components. These only appear when the full wired pipeline runs end-to-end with real module interactions. A unit test with a controlled scorer cannot expose non-determinism injected by other modules. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed; an in-process stack is sufficient to expose determinism failures. LLM-DEP: The system is described as a traditional weighing algorithm; if it used a real LLM the test would always fail (Agentic), but the behavior asserts determinism is expected, implying the system is designed to be deterministic. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: the behavior requires determinism, which disqualifies using a real LLM (whose outputs are non-deterministic); this test validates the algorithmic pipeline's determinism, not LLM output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Fix a user state (profile, browsing history, purchase history) and catalog snapshot. Execute the recommendation pipeline twice with identical inputs and no mutations between calls. Assert the two result lists are identical in length, product IDs, and rank order. Run this assertion across at least three distinct user profiles (new user, moderate history, heavy history) to cover different algorithm branches.
